records.title: Understand primary and secondary school stages
records.description: Dutch school education moves from primary education into differentiated secondary pathways, with school boards responsible for admission policies and families comparing schools, support and local arrangements.
records.ai_summary: Start school orientation early, read each school guide and ask about admission, language support, care and waiting lists. Registration with a municipality does not automatically enrol a child in a school.

records.title: Compare VMBO, HAVO and VWO pathways
records.description: Secondary education includes VMBO, HAVO and VWO routes with different durations, curricula and progression options toward MBO, HBO or research university education.
records.ai_summary: Discuss the school recommendation, available bridge classes and progression routes with the primary and secondary school. A pathway is important but later movement and further study routes can remain possible.

records.title: Follow compulsory education and qualification duties
records.description: Children living in the Netherlands generally must attend school from the applicable age and continue education until the compulsory or qualification-duty endpoint is reached.
records.ai_summary: Register a child with a suitable school promptly and report moves or attendance problems to the school and municipality. Do not assume home circumstances or travel automatically create an exemption.

records.title: Request or report authorised school absence
records.description: Illness, important family events, education abroad and certain exceptional circumstances have specific reporting or exemption routes through the school head or municipality.
records.ai_summary: Notify illness within the school's deadline and request planned leave in writing before absence. For broader exemptions, confirm the responsible authority and required evidence with the municipality.

records.title: Arrange appropriate education and additional support
records.description: Schools have a duty of care to seek an appropriate educational place for pupils who need extra support, whether at the current school, another mainstream school or special education.
records.ai_summary: Describe support needs during application and ask for the school's support profile and written decisions. Keep assessment and communication records when another placement or specialist help is proposed.

records.title: Understand primary-school costs and voluntary contributions
records.description: Primary education has no tuition fee, while schools may request voluntary contributions for extras and parents may separately pay for childcare or certain supervision arrangements.
records.ai_summary: Read the school guide to separate required learning materials from voluntary extras. A child must not be excluded from school-organised extra activities solely because a voluntary contribution was not paid.

records.title: Understand secondary-school materials and costs
records.description: Secondary education does not charge ordinary tuition to school-age pupils and schools provide most textbooks, while families may pay for personal materials and genuinely voluntary extras.
records.ai_summary: Ask the school for an itemised materials list and identify which costs are mandatory, personal or voluntary. Verify separate rules when a learner is 18 or attends adult or vocational education.

records.title: Check official school-holiday regions and dates
records.description: Primary and secondary school holidays are distributed across national regions, with fixed main holiday periods and advisory dates that schools publish in their own calendars.
records.ai_summary: Identify the school's holiday region and compare national dates with its own school guide. Do not book travel outside the published calendar without written permission where required.

records.title: Use the formal school complaint route
records.description: Parents and pupils should normally raise a concern with the teacher or school leadership before using the school board, confidential adviser or complaints committee named in the school guide.
records.ai_summary: Write down the facts, desired resolution and responses, then follow the school's published complaint process in order. Serious safety, discrimination or abuse concerns can require specialist reporting routes.

records.title: Check school quality and the role of the inspectorate
records.description: The Inspectorate of Education supervises educational quality and publishes reports, while individual complaints usually remain with the school complaint route unless a specific reporting function applies.
records.ai_summary: Use inspection reports as one input when comparing schools, and address personal disputes through the school's formal route. Contact a confidential inspector for the serious categories identified by the authority.
